rationale: The citing paper uses Lee and Labroo (2004) in its core conceptual argument for why logo-brand congruence should influence attitudes: congruent pairings are expected to raise conceptual fluency, which then improves evaluations. This is not just a background name-check; it is cited as direct prior evidence for the fluency-to-evaluation link during hypothesis development. The paper later reiterates this dependence in discussing limitations and future work on testing fluency more directly. Although it does not reuse Lee and Labroo's exact manipulations, the citation meaningfully informs the mechanism framing.
relation_description: The citing paper relies on Lee and Labroo (2004) as conceptual support that higher conceptual fluency can produce more favorable brand evaluations. It uses that result as a foundation for proposing that congruence between logo dynamism and brand characteristics improves attitudes via a fluency-based account.
